textPlain: As a professional, I've always disliked this phrase.  It seems like it's just tailor made to sound counterintuitive to pique interest, but in my opinion it actually means very little in the context of Keplerian motion.Boosting retrograde (slowing down) will lower your opposing apsis, increasing the speed on the other end of your orbit.  This much is true.  But speed is only one component of orbital motion, and is almost more akin to position in the orbital regime.  A spacecrafts speed at a given position will dictate it's trajectory

I have a hard time imagining physics. For example take a train moving 100 kmh to the north which wants to reverse direction to the south. It has to break and then accelerate again, a very costly operation. Except when the tracks make a turn? But how can a northward momentum change to a southward momentum?The same confusion I have when trying to imagine satellites going around Earth or slingshot maneuvers. Would an X-Wing turn in space differently than in the atmosphere of Hoth? Would it in space just rotate, but keep its forward (now backwards) momentum instead of turning like a fighter jet?
They should really teach physics using KSP.

For anyone who is remotely interested in this, a considerable chunk of the Gemini program was all about solving some of the practicalities involved with Rendezvous, and it is quite interesting even hearing some of the astronauts come to grips with some of the physics while orbiting in space trying the various types of rendezvous and docking maneuvers that were attempted.
Forwards is up, up is back, back is down, down is forwards.
A similar thing is true when cornering a race car when measuring time through the corner.
I thought I understood all this until I played Kerbal Space Program.

This is a consequence of the virial theorem of mechanics.https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Virial_theoremThis theorem also lets you conclude that as a nondegenerate star becomes more tightly bound (smaller, for a given mass) it must also become hotter.
In your train example, the rails exert a force on the train as it turns. In orbit, the planets are constantly exerting a force on the satellite.
play KSP, it will click after a few days.

Yeah, it's amazing. With enough docking and maneuvering practice I developed some kind of intuition for moving in space. I could maneuver without meticulously planning the burns.Still can't leave Eve though...
I wish ksp 2 hadn't been a boondoggle
I tried to teach a group of HS students about orbital mechanics as a high school physics teacher using KSP. It was... difficult. Not impossible. But I agree it's an excellent learning tool.

I'm a professional astrodynamicist and I owe my base level understanding of orbital mechanics to KSP.  It's a fantastic resource for learning the basics of Keplerian motion.Also, obligatory XKCD: https://xkcd.com/1356/
I doubt SpaceX could put a satellite in orbit with KSP physics. Just the absence of realistic thermal conduction would prevent it. They might as well do their calculations with pi equal to 3.
